title: Introduction to Differential Forms + Exercises
description: A first serious MathLesson pilot lesson on differential forms: covectors, wedge products, exterior derivative, pullbacks, integration, Stokes' theorem, exact and closed forms, with exercises and quiz questions.

sections.label: 1. Why forms?
sections.title: Why differential forms?
sections.content:
Why Differential Forms?

Differential forms are the language of oriented measurement. They generalize the basic objects that appear in calculus: gradients, line integrals, flux integrals, curls, divergences, and change of variables.

Core idea
A differential form is a field of alternating multilinear measurement rules. A $k$-form eats $k$ tangent vectors and returns a signed scalar.
DegreeObjectInputGeometric measurement
$0$function $f$a pointscalar value
$1$covector field $P\,dx+Q\,dy$one tangent vectororiented length component / work density
$2$area form $F\,dx\wedge dy$two tangent vectorsoriented area density / flux density
$3$volume form $G\,dx\wedge dy\wedge dz$three tangent vectorsoriented volume density
Differential forms as oriented measurements
flowchart LR
  A["Function"] --> B["0-form"]
  C["Line density"] --> D["1-form"]
  E["Area density"] --> F["2-form"]
  G["Volume density"] --> H["3-form"]
  B --> I["Exterior derivative"]
  D --> I
  F --> I
Prototype in the plane
$$\omega=P(x,y)\,dx+Q(x,y)\,dy.$$

At a point $(x,y)$, the $1$-form above acts on a tangent vector $v=(a,b)$ by

$$\omega_{(x,y)}(v)=P(x,y)a+Q(x,y)b.$$
Let $\omega=2x\,dx+y\,dy$. Evaluate $\omega_{(1,4)}(3,-1)$.

sections.label: 2. Covectors
sections.title: Covectors and one-forms
sections.content:
Covectors and One-Forms

A vector points in a direction. A covector measures a component of a vector. The basic coordinate covectors are $dx$, $dy$, and $dz$.

Coordinate covectors
On $\mathbb{R}^2$, if $v=a\,\partial_x+b\,\partial_y$, then
$$dx(v)=a,\qquad dy(v)=b.$$
Thus $dx$ extracts the $x$-component and $dy$ extracts the $y$-component.

A general $1$-form in the plane is

$$\omega=P(x,y)\,dx+Q(x,y)\,dy.$$
Example: a work form
A force field $F=(P,Q)$ in the plane corresponds to the work $1$-form
$$\omega_F=P\,dx+Q\,dy.$$
The line integral $\int_C \omega_F$ is the work done along the oriented curve $C$.
Vector calculus objectDifferential-form versionTypical integral
scalar field $f$$0$-form $f$evaluation or derivative
force field $(P,Q)$$1$-form $P\,dx+Q\,dy$$\int_C P\,dx+Q\,dy$
density field $\rho$$2$-form $\rho\,dx\wedge dy$$\int_R \rho\,dx\wedge dy$
Let $\omega=x\,dx-3y\,dy$. Evaluate $\omega_{(2,1)}(1,-2)$.

sections.label: 3. Wedge product
sections.title: The wedge product
sections.content:
The Wedge Product

The wedge product builds higher-degree forms from lower-degree forms. Its defining feature is antisymmetry: swapping two inputs changes the sign.

Antisymmetry
$$dx\wedge dy=-dy\wedge dx,\qquad dx\wedge dx=0.$$
Area from two vectors
For vectors $u=(a,b)$ and $v=(c,d)$ in $\mathbb{R}^2$,
$$(dx\wedge dy)(u,v)=\begin{vmatrix}a&c\\ b&d\end{vmatrix}=ad-bc.$$
This is the signed area of the parallelogram spanned by $u$ and $v$.
Why this matters
The wedge product remembers orientation. The expression $dx\wedge dy$ treats the ordered pair $(\partial_x,\partial_y)$ as positive and $(\partial_y,\partial_x)$ as negative.
Compute $(dx\wedge dy)((0,1),(1,0))$.

sections.label: 4. Exterior derivative
sections.title: The exterior derivative
sections.content:
The Exterior Derivative

The exterior derivative is the fundamental differentiation operator for differential forms. It raises degree by one:

$$d:\Omega^k\longrightarrow \Omega^{k+1}.$$
Exterior derivative ladder
flowchart LR
  F["0-forms: functions"] -->|"d"| O["1-forms"]
  O -->|"d"| T["2-forms"]
  T -->|"d"| H["3-forms"]
  H -->|"d"| Z["0 in R3"]
Derivative of a function
For a smooth function $f(x,y)$,
$$df=f_x\,dx+f_y\,dy.$$
This is the differential form version of the gradient, but written as a covector field.
Derivative of a plane one-form
If $\omega=P\,dx+Q\,dy$, then
$$d\omega=(Q_x-P_y)\,dx\wedge dy.$$
This is the scalar curl expression in differential-form notation.
Structural rule
The exterior derivative always satisfies
$$d(d\alpha)=0.$$
This single identity contains familiar facts such as curl grad $=0$ and div curl $=0$.
Let $\omega=y^2\,dx+x^2\,dy$. Enter the coefficient of $dx\wedge dy$ in $d\omega$.

sections.label: 5. Pullbacks
sections.title: Pullbacks and parametrization
sections.content:
Pullbacks and Parametrization

Forms are designed to be integrated over parametrized curves and surfaces. The mechanism is the pullback.

Pullback intuition
If $\phi:U\to M$ is a parametrization and $\omega$ is a form on $M$, the pullback $\phi^*\omega$ is the form on the parameter domain $U$ obtained by substituting the parametrization and its differentials.

For a curve $\gamma(t)=(x(t),y(t))$ and a $1$-form $\omega=P\,dx+Q\,dy$,

$$\gamma^*\omega=\left(P(x(t),y(t))x'(t)+Q(x(t),y(t))y'(t)\right)dt.$$
Example
Let $\gamma(t)=(t,t^2)$ and $\omega=y\,dx+x\,dy$. Then $x=t$, $y=t^2$, $dx=dt$, and $dy=2t\,dt$, so
$$\gamma^*\omega=t^2\,dt+t(2t\,dt)=3t^2\,dt.$$
Pullback workflow
flowchart LR
  A["Form on target"] --> B["Choose parametrization"]
  B --> C["Substitute coordinates"]
  C --> D["Substitute differentials"]
  D --> E["Integrate on parameter domain"]
For $\gamma(t)=(t,t^2)$ and $\omega=y\,dx+x\,dy$, enter the coefficient of $dt$ in $\gamma^*\omega$.

sections.label: 6. Line integrals
sections.title: Integrating one-forms over curves
sections.content:
Integrating One-Forms Over Curves

Line integrals become simple once the $1$-form is pulled back to the parameter interval.

Line integral of a one-form
$$\int_C \omega=\int_a^b \gamma^*\omega.$$
Example: direct calculation
Let $\omega=y\,dx+x\,dy$ and let $C$ be $\gamma(t)=(t,t^2)$ for $0\le t\le 1$. From the previous section,
$$\gamma^*\omega=3t^2\,dt.$$
Therefore
$$\int_C\omega=\int_0^1 3t^2\,dt=1.$$
Exact shortcut
Since $\omega=y\,dx+x\,dy=d(xy)$, the same integral equals
$$xy\big|_{(0,0)}^{(1,1)}=1-0=1.$$
Exact forms integrate by endpoints on curves.
Compute $\int_0^1 3t^2\,dt$.

sections.label: 7. Surface integrals
sections.title: Two-forms, surfaces, and orientation
sections.content:
Two-Forms, Surfaces, and Orientation

A $2$-form measures oriented area. On $\mathbb{R}^2$, a typical $2$-form is $F(x,y)\,dx\wedge dy$. On a surface in $\mathbb{R}^3$, $2$-forms encode flux-type quantities.

Plane area form
The form $dx\wedge dy$ is the standard positively oriented area form on the $xy$-plane. Reversing orientation changes the sign of the integral.
Area density integral
$$\int_R F(x,y)\,dx\wedge dy=\iint_R F(x,y)\,dA$$ when $R$ has the standard orientation.
Example
Over the unit square $[0,1]\times[0,1]$,
$$\int_R x\,dx\wedge dy=\int_0^1\int_0^1 x\,dy\,dx=\frac12.$$
Compute $\int_{[0,1]^2}x\,dx\wedge dy$.

sections.label: 8. Stokes
sections.title: The generalized Stokes theorem
sections.content:
The Generalized Stokes Theorem

The central theorem of differential forms is a single statement that contains the fundamental theorem of calculus, Green's theorem, Stokes' theorem, and the divergence theorem.

Generalized Stokes theorem
$$\int_M d\omega=\int_{\partial M}\omega.$$
Meaning
The integral of a derivative over a region equals the integral of the original form over the boundary, provided the orientations are compatible.
Classical theorems as cases of Stokes
flowchart LR
  A["General Stokes"] --> B["Fundamental theorem"]
  A --> C["Green theorem"]
  A --> D["Vector Stokes theorem"]
  A --> E["Divergence theorem"]
Dimension of $M$Form degreeClassical theorem
$1$$0$-formFundamental theorem of calculus
$2$$1$-formGreen's theorem / planar curl theorem
$3$ surface in $\mathbb{R}^3$$1$-form on surfaceStokes' curl theorem
$3$ region$2$-formDivergence theorem
In $\int_M d\omega=\int_{\partial M}\omega$, the symbol $\partial M$ represents the ______ of $M$.

sections.label: 9. Closed and exact
sections.title: Closed forms, exact forms, and first cohomology intuition
sections.content:
Closed Forms, Exact Forms, and First Cohomology Intuition

The identity $d^2=0$ implies that every exact form is closed. The converse may fail, and that failure measures topology.

Closed and exact
A form $\omega$ is closed if $d\omega=0$. It is exact if there exists a form $\eta$ such that $\omega=d\eta$.
Exact implies closed
$$\omega=d\eta\quad\Longrightarrow\quad d\omega=d(d\eta)=0.$$
A closed but not globally exact example
On the punctured plane $\mathbb{R}^2\setminus\{0\}$, the form
$$\omega=\frac{-y}{x^2+y^2}\,dx+\frac{x}{x^2+y^2}\,dy$$
is closed but not globally exact. Its integral around the unit circle is $2\pi$.
Topology enters through exactness failure
flowchart LR
  A["Exact"] --> B["Closed"]
  B --> C["Locally potential-like"]
  B --> D["May fail globally"]
  D --> E["Topology detected by periods"]
If $\omega=d\eta$, what is $d\omega$?

sections.label: 10. Worked review
sections.title: Worked review exercises
sections.content:
Worked Review Exercises

This section consolidates the main skills: evaluate one-forms, compute wedge products, compute exterior derivatives, pull back along curves, and interpret Stokes' theorem.

Exercise A: exterior derivative
Let $\omega=(x^2y)\,dx+(xy^2)\,dy$. Then
$$d\omega=\left(\frac{\partial}{\partial x}(xy^2)-\frac{\partial}{\partial y}(x^2y)\right)dx\wedge dy=(y^2-x^2)\,dx\wedge dy.$$
For $\omega=x^2y\,dx+xy^2\,dy$, enter the coefficient of $dx\wedge dy$ in $d\omega$.
Exercise B: pullback
Let $\gamma(t)=(\cos t,\sin t)$ and $\omega=-y\,dx+x\,dy$. Since $dx=-\sin t\,dt$ and $dy=\cos t\,dt$,
$$\gamma^*\omega=-(\sin t)(-\sin t\,dt)+(\cos t)(\cos t\,dt)=dt.$$
Thus $\int_0^{2\pi}\gamma^*\omega=2\pi$.
For $\gamma(t)=(\cos t,\sin t)$ and $\omega=-y\,dx+x\,dy$, enter the coefficient of $dt$ in $\gamma^*\omega$.
Mental model
Differential forms are not just notation. They encode what is being measured, how orientation matters, and why the same theorem appears in many versions of vector calculus.

quiz.stem: A differential $1$-form primarily acts on how many tangent vectors?
quiz.feedback: A $1$-form is a covector field, so it acts on one tangent vector.

quiz.stem: Which identity expresses the antisymmetry of the wedge product?
quiz.feedback: The wedge product is alternating, so swapping factors changes the sign.

quiz.stem: For every form $\alpha$, the exterior derivative satisfies $d(d\alpha)=0$.
If $\omega=P\,dx+Q\,dy$ on $\mathbb{R}^2$, then $d\omega$ equals which expression?
quiz.feedback: The exterior derivative of $P\,dx+Q\,dy$ is $(Q_x-P_y)\,dx\wedge dy$.

quiz.stem: Let $f(x,y)=x^2+y^2$. Enter the coefficient of $dx$ in $df$.

quiz.hint: This is the determinant of the identity matrix.
quiz.feedback: The ordered basis $(\partial_x,\partial_y)$ has positive orientation, so the value is $1$.

quiz.stem: In the generalized Stokes theorem $\int_M d\omega=\int_{\partial M}\omega$, what is integrated on the boundary?
quiz.feedback: The original form $\omega$ is integrated over the boundary $\partial M$.

quiz.stem: Every closed form is globally exact on every domain.
quiz.feedback: Closed forms need not be globally exact on domains with nontrivial topology, such as a punctured plane.

quiz.stem: Let $\gamma(t)=(t,t^2)$ and $\omega=y\,dx+x\,dy$. Enter the coefficient of $dt$ in $\gamma^*\omega$.
